best_contact_id: ## [Be the CMO Everyone Wants to Work With](https://kellblog.com/2026/03/21/how-to-be-the-cmo-everyone-wants-to-work-with/)

company.blog_url: 2026](https://kellblog.com/2026/03/21/how-to-be-the-cmo-everyone-wants-to-work-with/ "7:48 am") | [Leave a comment](https://kellblog.com/2026/03/21/how-to-be-the-cmo-everyone-wants-to-work-with/#respond)

best_contact_id: I’m a big fan of Dave Gerhardt and the [Exit Five](https://www.exitfive.com/) marketing community he has built. While I have always liked the idea of peer-networking communities
company.blog_url: I think they change from vitamin to painkiller in times of rapid change. Why? Because the playbooks haven’t been written yet.
for example. Today
company.careers_url: you can find scores of blogs — including mine — that talk about how to run SaaS businesses
company.description: and how to produce and interpret SaaS metrics. Twenty years ago virtually none of that existed. You needed to figure it out. And one of the best ways to figure it out was to spend time with other people who were doing the same figuring.

best_contact_id: There’s a time for timeless wisdom and there’s a time for talking to other people who are doing the same thing as you are
company.blog_url: right now. I think AI and the massive disruption it creates in marketing — from content to workflow to performance marketing to analytics — means it’s an awesome time to join a marketing peer-networking community.

best_contact_id: I tell this to literally every CMO I work with:
* Come to me for timeless wisdom (if not fleeting opinions).
* Join Exit Five (or equivalent) to talk to peers who face the same challenges you do
company.blog_url: every day.

best_contact_id: That’s not to say that following thought leaders isn’t a great tactic
company.blog_url: too. I keep an eye on Emily Kramer
company.careers_url: Elena Verna
company.employee_count: and Jon Miller. And I can’t wait for Rand Fishkin’s rumored new book on Zero-Click Marketing. If there are other marketing thought leaders you think I should be following

best_contact_id: All this is why I was thrilled when Dave Gerhardt invited me to speak at Exit Five’s Marketing Leadership Retreat on March 19-20 in Phoenix. Given the above
company.blog_url: I knew I wasn’t going to be the person delivering fresh-from-the-trenches information on AI tools and methods. The audience is 100x more qualified than I am to do that.

best_contact_id: So what did I want to offer up instead? Some timeless wisdom. Specifically
company.blog_url: timeless wisdom not just on how to successfully do the CMO job
company.careers_url: but on how to be the CMO everyone wants to work with.

best_contact_id: Why that topic? And bear in mind it takes a lot for me to pick a title that ends with a preposition. Because I thought it captured the key to success in an important way.
* **It’s not just about doing the job**. Yes
company.blog_url: that’s quite hard already but if you only focus on that you ironically increase your odds of becoming a statistic.

best_contact_id: * **It’s not just about keeping the job**. And yes
company.blog_url: there’s an art to that
company.careers_url: a big part of that is simply remembering to market marketing.

best_contact_id: * **It’s about helping the boss do their job** which not only increases your strategic value
company.blog_url: but makes you more “sticky” in your role.

best_contact_id: * It’s about doing all that **while being the CMO that everyone wants to work with** (EW2WW)
Why does that last point matter?

company.blog_url: so a strong [internal customer service](https://kellblog.com/2020/04/26/marketing-exists-to-make-sales-easier/) orientation never hurts.

best_contact_id: * Being the CMO EW2WW **helps you keep your job**. With a median tenure of 18-24 months
company.blog_url: this should never be too far from a CMO’s mind. Belt and suspenders.

best_contact_id: * If _everyone_ wants to work with you
company.blog_url: **it helps you find your next job**. Board members and recruiters — your top two job sources after peers — will seek you out when the time comes.

best_contact_id: * It might well **help you get promoted to COO or CEO**. If everyone wants to work with you
company.blog_url: they might well give you a shot at the next level.

best_contact_id: * **It frames things as a positioning problem**. And we know a lot about solving positioning problems
company.blog_url: working backwards from a desired result.
I’m saying that CMOs should want to position themselves as the CMO EW2WW.
position thyself.** ”

best_contact_id: When the auto industry was being decimated and jobs offshored to Japan

if Japan is better at making cars than we are
company.careers_url: then they should make cars and we should do other things. It’ll be rough for the displaced auto workers but it’s merely a transition period and
company.description: it’ll be a win/win for both countries and we’ll all be better off in the end.

company.blog_url: national security

company.company_type: we typically don’t miss the jobs displaced by technology.

company.careers_url: pin setters

company.industry: as a society we don’t miss them. Most coal miners work hard so their children won’t have to be coal miners._

best_contact_id: The pain is real and while occupational transfers are a solution

_Let’s remember where the word Luddite comes from. Today
company.blog_url: we use the word to describe people opposed to new technology. But the Luddites were part of a**labor** movement; they didn’t destroy machines because they were technophobes

company.blog_url: with our safe emotional distance from a 1970s auto worker

ride this one to the beach and go retire.
you need to switch horses.

best_contact_id: * Switching early is better than switching late. The goal is to get onto the next horse before everyone else realizes the race has moved.
The popular horse-switching fantasy answer is retraining. “Go back to school and become an engineer.” In theory
company.blog_url: yes. In practice
company.careers_url: rarely. The jump from an assembly-line worker to an engineer requires years of schooling and a different educational foundation.

best_contact_id: The real move for the autoworker was sideways
company.blog_url: not upward: industrial maintenance
company.careers_url: tool and die work

best_contact_id: None of these are glamorous answers
company.blog_url: but they share an important property: they’re adjacent to the skill set the person already has. They’re realistic.
without the emotional distance we have from a 1970s auto worker
company.careers_url: let’s try applying the same thinking to ourselves. One way to do that is to imagine what someone from the year 2070 might write back to us.

best_contact_id: They might start the same way.

company.blog_url: it’s a trend. Don’t assume this wave of AI capability will conveniently stall just below your job description. You can try to fight your way through it
company.careers_url: but the odds will not be forever in your favor. The job you’re defending may still exist

company.careers_url: you should be thinking about switching horses.

best_contact_id: But here the analogy gets interesting.

company.blog_url: by contrast

this all hits different because **this time it’s us**. That’s where we lose the emotional distance

best_contact_id: Citrini’s recent [thought experiment](https://www.citriniresearch.com/p/2028gic) imagines a negative feedback loop with no natural brake. As they describe it:

best_contact_id: The one thing I find most missing from the AI future analyses I’ve read is a simple realization: in my experience

company.blog_url: I’ve been asked countless times whether the market was saturated or saturating.

best_contact_id: My answer was always the same.
One day I’ll go to a cocktail party and ask everyone in the room: “Does everybody have all the information they need to make data-informed management decisions?” If everyone says yes
company.blog_url: then on that day the BI market will be saturated.

best_contact_id: But that day strikes me as a long way off. It did 40 years ago and it still does today. We have accomplished so
company.blog_url: so much in BI and analytics. But there is still so much more to do.

best_contact_id: Since no AI post would be complete without a reference to [Jevons Paradox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jevons_paradox)
company.blog_url: let’s invoke it here: When technology makes something cheaper or more efficient
company.careers_url: we tend to use more of it
company.company_type: not less. When steam engines became more efficient
company.description: coal consumption didn’t fall — it exploded because steam power became economical in far more applications. The same dynamic has repeated itself with computing
company.evidence_urls: and bandwidth. Every time technology dramatically increases our ability to do something
company.founded_year: we don’t run out of work. We discover many more things worth doing.

best_contact_id: The vast majority of management and strategic consulting today is about focus. And the reason focus matters so much is simple: execution bandwidth is limited. Organizations can only get so many things right.
So managers spend an enormous amount of time cutting
company.blog_url: cutting
company.careers_url: and cutting. Prioritizing
company.employee_count: if we had to list the one key to business success
company.evidence_urls: it’s focus. Why? Because there is so much work that we could do. So many strategies that we can imagine. So many things that we could do to help our customers.

best_contact_id: It’s not that there’s _only a little_ more work to do. There is an unfathomable amount of work yet to be done. The job of management is pruning that list down to something the organization can actually execute.

Which is why the idea that we’re somehow going to run out of work strikes me as absurd. It feels like a theory written by people who haven’t actually spent much time doing the work in the first place — serving customers
company.blog_url: building products
company.careers_url: and running businesses. There is _always_ more that could be done.

company.blog_url: the people who say SaaS will stand for “service as a software” are the same people saying AI will massively disrupt employment. But when you sell service-as-a-software you are definitionally in the solutions business
company.careers_url: solving problems

company.description: ” you quickly discover that the list is very

company.blog_url: I’m not particularly worried about us running out of work to do.

company.careers_url: given everything we’ve said thus far
